title: Propagation-mediated amplification of \{11\={2}0\}-biased inversion domain boundary alignment in polarity-mixed GaN lateral overgrowth
summary: GaN polarity inversion and the associated inversion domain boundaries (IDBs) are frequently observed during lateral overgrowth and are often discussed in terms of the small energetic spread among competing IDB structures predicted by first-principles calculations. In circular mask openings, \(\{11\bar{2}0\}\)-aligned IDBs have previously been explained by geometric closure of a single-polarity hexagonal domain at the circular boundary. Here we examine an experimentally distinct regime in which opposite-polarity domains already coexist within the opening before the later development of long, straight IDB traces. In this mixed-polarity regime, the final trace orientation cannot be attributed solely to the macroscopic circular boundary. Nevertheless, plan-view SEM line-trace statistics show that IDB orientations remain biased toward the \(\{11\bar{2}0\}\) family. To quantify how this bias develops during propagation, we perform distance-resolved, length-weighted orientation analysis in concentric annular regions defined from the opening center. The resulting metrics show that \(\{11\bar{2}0\}\)-biased alignment is progressively amplified with propagation distance, while the orientation distribution becomes narrower, indicating systematic sharpening of the preferred alignment state. We further apply the same ring-resolved statistical operators to minimal two-domain propagation simulations in a circular opening and find that a propagation-mediated anisotropy reproduces the observed radial amplification under fixed circular geometry. Together, these results establish a quantitative phenomenology of \(\{11\bar{2}0\}\)-biased IDB alignment in polarity-mixed GaN lateral overgrowth on patterned sapphire and indicate that, although mask-boundary-imposed selection may describe single-polarity closure cases, the present mixed-polarity regime is better explained by propagation-mediated amplification.

AI.tldr: This study investigates Gallium Nitride (GaN) polarity inversion and inversion domain boundaries (IDBs) during lateral overgrowth on patterned sapphire. In a mixed-polarity regime where opposite-polarity domains coexist within mask openings, researchers discovered that IDB orientations remain biased toward the {11̅20̅} family, with this bias progressively amplifying during propagation. Through experimental analysis and simulation, they demonstrate that propagation-mediated amplification, rather than mask-boundary geometry, explains the observed IDB alignment behavior.
AI.motivation: While {11̅20̅}-aligned IDBs in circular mask openings have been previously attributed to geometric closure of a single-polarity hexagonal domain at the circular boundary, this study addresses an experimentally distinct regime where opposite-polarity domains already coexist within the opening before IDB development, making the macroscopic circular boundary an insufficient explanation.
AI.method: Plan-view SEM line-trace statistics combined with distance-resolved, length-weighted orientation analysis in concentric annular regions defined from the opening center. The same statistical operators were applied to minimal two-domain propagation simulations in a circular opening.
AI.result: The study found that {11̅20̅}-biased alignment is progressively amplified with propagation distance, while the orientation distribution becomes narrower, indicating systematic sharpening. Simulation results confirm that propagation-mediated anisotropy reproduces the observed radial amplification under fixed circular geometry.
AI.conclusion: Propagation-mediated amplification, rather than mask-boundary-imposed selection, explains the {11̅20̅}-biased IDB alignment in mixed-polarity GaN lateral overgrowth. The bias progressively strengthens with propagation distance while the orientation distribution narrows, indicating systematic sharpening of the preferred alignment state.

title: Stress Asymmetry in Hard Magnetic Soft Materials
summary: Hard magnetic soft materials -- soft polymers embedded with hard magnetic particles -- are modeled using continuum magnetomechanical formulations in which the deformation and the magnetization field are the primary kinematic variables. A recent question in such formulations is whether the Cauchy stress is symmetric, which is directly related to frame invariance and angular momentum balance. This note discusses energetically equivalent formulations, related by a change of variables between referential and current descriptions of the magnetization, and shows that they generally yield different Cauchy stresses, including a change in their symmetry. Specifically, the formulation based on a referential magnetization produces a symmetric Cauchy stress, while that based on a current magnetization generally yields an asymmetric Cauchy stress. We highlight that when the internal variable (magnetization field) is at the energy-minimizing equilibrium configuration, the divergences of these stresses are the same, and both stresses are symmetric.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates whether the Cauchy stress is symmetric in continuum magnetomechanical formulations of hard magnetic soft materials, showing that different magnetization descriptions yield different stress symmetries except at energy-minimizing equilibrium.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to resolve a recent question in modeling hard magnetic soft materials: whether the Cauchy stress is symmetric, which is directly tied to frame invariance and angular momentum balance - fundamental physical consistency requirements.
AI.method: The authors use continuum magnetomechanical formulations with deformation and magnetization as primary kinematic variables. They analyze energetically equivalent formulations related by a change of variables between referential and current descriptions of the magnetization.
AI.result: The key result is that these formulations generally yield different Cauchy stresses - the referential magnetization-based formulation produces a symmetric Cauchy stress, while the current magnetization-based formulation generally yields an asymmetric Cauchy stress. However, when the magnetization field is at energy-minimizing equilibrium configuration, both stresses become symmetric and have the same divergence.
AI.conclusion: The conclusion clarifies that the apparent inconsistency in stress symmetry arises from the choice of magnetization description (referential vs current), but this discrepancy vanishes at the physically relevant equilibrium state where both formulations agree.

title: Spatially modulated morphotropic phase boundaries in a compressively strained multiferroic thin film
summary: The coexisting rhombohedral-like (R', MA) and tetragonal-like (T', MC) monoclinic phases in compressively strained bismuth ferrite thin films exhibit exceptional piezoelectric and magnetic properties. While previous studies have largely focused on probing the morphotropic phase boundaries (MPBs) comprising ordered R'/T' twins, their self-organizing structures remain not fully explored. Here, we observed two types of interphase boundaries in a 60 nm-thick BiFeO3 film epitaxially grown on a LaAlO3 substrate by employing multi-modal diffraction-based electron microscopy. First, the flat MPBs form lines extending >1 mm, and repeat almost every ~20 um. Additionally, we uncover a new type of phase boundary with zig-zag regions of alternating R'/R' and T'/T' twin domains. Cross-sectional multislice electron ptychography confirms the atomic-scale polarization rotation across the MPB, with out-of-plane strain varying >15%. Plan-view electron backscatter diffraction reveals the lattice disclination of ~1.5-degrees across the zig-zag interphase boundaries, while having >2.5 degrees within the MPB. Phase-field modeling suggests that the formation of zig-zag phase boundaries arises from balancing between Landau and elastic energies. We speculate that such well-ordered interphase boundaries are associated with mesoscale in-plane strain modulations, thus providing a way to engineer and harness their properties for potential device applications.

AI.tldr: Discovery of two types of interphase boundaries in compressively strained BiFeO3 thin films: conventional flat MPBs extending >1 mm and novel zig-zag phase boundaries with alternating twin domains, characterized by atomic-scale polarization rotation and significant lattice disclination.
AI.motivation: While previous studies have largely focused on probing the morphotropic phase boundaries (MPBs) comprising ordered R'/T' twins in bismuth ferrite thin films, their self-organizing structures remain not fully explored.
AI.method: Multi-modal diffraction-based electron microscopy including cross-sectional multislice electron ptychography and plan-view electron backscatter diffraction, combined with phase-field modeling. The study examined a 60 nm-thick BiFeO3 film epitaxially grown on LaAlO3 substrate.
AI.result: Two types of interphase boundaries were observed: (1) flat MPBs forming lines extending >1 mm, repeating almost every ~20 um; (2) a new type of phase boundary with zig-zag regions of alternating R'/R' and T'/T' twin domains. Cross-sectional multislice electron ptychography confirmed atomic-scale polarization rotation across MPB with out-of-plane strain varying >15%. Plan-view EBSD revealed lattice disclination of ~1.5-degrees across zig-zag interphase boundaries, while having >2.5 degrees within the MPB.
AI.conclusion: The zig-zag phase boundaries arise from balancing Landau and elastic energies, and are associated with mesoscale in-plane strain modulations. These well-ordered interphase boundaries provide a way to engineer and harness their properties for potential device applications.

title: Metallic d-wave altermagnetism in WFeB: a platform for electrically switchable perpendicular spin-splitter response
summary: We report the synthesis and magnetic characterization of WFeB and identify it as a metallic d-wave altermagnet representative of a broader TiNiSi-type family. Neutron diffraction, Mössbauer spectroscopy, and magnetometry establish a collinear altermagnetic ordering confirmed by first-principles calculations. The electronic structure shows a nonrelativistic spin splitting of approximately 100 meV, but it also supports a strong spin-splitter transport response. This demonstrates that efficient spin-current generation can occur even with such modest band splitting. Symmetry analysis shows that selected film orientations permit deterministic switching of the Néel vector by current-induced staggered torques, enabling electrical control of a perpendicular spin-splitter response. These results establish WFeB and related TiNiSi-type antiferromagnets as a platform for electrically switchable charge-to-spin conversion driven by altermagnetic symmetry.

AI.tldr: This paper reports the synthesis and characterization of WFeB as a new d-wave altermagnetic material in the TiNiSi-type family, demonstrating efficient spin-current generation despite modest band splitting and enabling electrical control of spin response through current-induced torques.
AI.motivation: The research aims to develop new materials for spintronic applications that can efficiently generate spin currents through altermagnetic symmetry, enabling electrical control of spin-polarized transport.
AI.method: Neutron diffraction, Mössbauer spectroscopy, magnetometry, and first-principles calculations were used to synthesize and characterize WFeB. The study involved symmetry analysis to determine film orientations enabling deterministic switching of the Néel vector by current-induced staggered torques.
AI.result: WFeB was identified as a metallic d-wave altermagnet in the TiNiSi-type family. It exhibits a nonrelativistic spin splitting of approximately 100 meV but demonstrates strong spin-splitter transport response, enabling efficient spin-current generation. Selected film orientations allow deterministic switching of the Néel vector by current-induced staggered torques, permitting electrical control of a perpendicular spin-splitter response.
AI.conclusion: WFeB and related TiNiSi-type antiferromagnets represent a promising platform for electrically switchable charge-to-spin conversion driven by altermagnetic symmetry. This work establishes a new family of materials for spintronic applications where deterministic control of spin currents can be achieved through current-induced torques.

title: Crystals Caught Doping: Metallic Wigner Crystals in Rhombohedral Graphene
summary: Nearly a century after Wigner's initial proposal, electron crystals are now a topic of intense experimental and theoretical interest. However, most proposed crystalline phases are commensurate and therefore become insulating in the presence of even weak pinning. In this work we discuss when a commensurate Wigner crystal will spontaneously self dope and develop itinerant carriers, giving rise to an incommensurate and thus metallic Wigner crystal (MWC). We develop a general criterion for the instability of the commensurate crystal which involves the competition between the charge gap at commensurability and a ``packing bias'' whose sign selects whether electron or hole doping is preferred. We then apply these insights to rhombohedral multilayer graphene, where calculations for commensurate crystals reveal instabilities towards self-doping. Carrying out self-consistent Hartree-Fock over the landscape of incommensurate crystals reveals the phase diagram, where a broad MWC phase appears directly adjacent to an insulating Wigner crystal phase. Recent observations of an island of reversed Hall conductance near a putative Wigner crystal phase in rhombohedral graphene are naturally explained by our theory.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses when commensurate Wigner crystals become unstable and self-dope to form metallic Wigner crystals (MWC), with application to rhombohedral graphene where such instabilities are predicted.
AI.motivation: Most proposed crystalline phases are commensurate and become insulating with even weak pinning. The authors seek to understand when these crystals will spontaneously develop itinerant carriers, creating metallic rather than insulating states.
AI.method: The authors develop a general instability criterion based on competition between charge gap at commensurability and a "packing bias" that determines electron vs hole doping preference. They apply this to rhombohedral multilayer graphene using self-consistent Hartree-Fock calculations.
AI.result: The theory reveals a broad MWC phase adjacent to the insulating Wigner crystal phase in the phase diagram. The predicted self-doping instability explains recent observations of reversed Hall conductance near the Wigner crystal phase in rhombohedral graphene.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a general framework for understanding metallic Wigner crystals and connects theory to experiment in rhombohedral graphene systems.

title: Directional-dependent Berezinskii-Kosterlitz-Thouless transition at EuO/KTaO$_3$(111) interfaces
comment: A combined file including main text (21 pages, 4 figures) and Supplementary Information (29 pages, 11 figures). Accepted by Communications Physics
summary: In two dimensions, a phase-coherent superconducting state is established via a Berezinskii-Kosterlitz-Thouless (BKT) transition, whose critical temperature $T_{\rm BKT}$ is determined by the global superfluid stiffness in uniform superconducting systems. We report that at the interface between (111)-oriented KTaO$_3$ and ferromagnetic EuO, the two-dimensional superconducting state exhibits a BKT transition relying on the direction of in-plane bias current. The highest $T_{\rm BKT}$ occurs when current is applied along one of the [11$\bar{2}$] axes of KTaO$_3$, underscoring a spontaneous breaking of the threefold lattice rotational symmetry. Such directional dependence of $T_{\rm BKT}$ is consistently reflected in the nonreciprocal signals stemming from superconducting fluctuations above the transition. We attribute this phenomenon to an interfacial phase segregation; the phase with higher $T_{\rm BKT}$ self-organizes into quasi-one-dimensional textures that stretch along one of the [11$\bar{2}$] directions. Our results point toward the emergence of exotic phases of matter beyond the description of conventional BKT physics at a superconducting interface that is subjected to ferromagnetic proximity.

AI.tldr: Discovery of direction-dependent BKT transition at the (111) KTaO3/EuO interface, where the highest T_BKT appears when current flows along [11-2] axes, revealing broken threefold symmetry due to interfacial phase segregation forming quasi-1D superconducting textures.
AI.motivation: Investigate the nature of Berezinskii-Kosterlitz-Thouless (BKT) transition in a two-dimensional superconducting state at the interface between (111)-oriented KTaO3 and ferromagnetic EuO, where unusual directional dependence of T_BKT was observed relative to in-plane bias current direction.
AI.method: Transport measurements with varying in-plane bias current directions on the (111)-oriented KTaO3/EuO interface; measured T_BKT and nonreciprocal signals from superconducting fluctuations above the transition temperature.
AI.result: Highest T_BKT occurs when current is applied along one of the [11-2] axes of KTaO3, revealing spontaneous breaking of threefold lattice rotational symmetry. This directional dependence is consistently reflected in nonreciprocal signals from superconducting fluctuations above T_BKT.
AI.conclusion: The phenomenon originates from interfacial phase segregation, where the higher T_BKT phase self-organizes into quasi-one-dimensional textures stretching along one of the [11-2] directions. This points toward exotic phases beyond conventional BKT physics at a superconducting interface under ferromagnetic proximity.

title: AI-assisted Human-in-the-Loop Web Platform for Structural Characterization in Hard drive design
summary: Scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) has become a cornerstone instrument for semiconductor materials metrology, enabling nanoscale analysis of complex multilayer structures that define device performance. Developing effective metrology workflows for such systems requires balancing automation with flexibility; rigid pipelines are brittle to sample variability, while purely manual approaches are slow and subjective. Here, we present a tunable human-AI-assisted workflow framework that enables modular and adaptive analysis of STEM images for device characterization. As an illustrative example, we demonstrate a workflow for automated layer thickness and interface roughness quantification in multilayer thin films. The system integrates gradient-based peak detection with interactive correction modules, allowing human input at the design stage while maintaining fully automated execution across samples. Implemented as a web-based interface, it processes TEM/EMD files directly, applies noise reduction and interface tracking algorithms, and outputs statistical roughness and thickness metrics with nanometer precision. This architecture exemplifies a general approach toward adaptive, reusable metrology workflows - bridging human insight and machine precision for scalable, standardized analysis in semiconductor manufacturing. The code is made available at https://github.com/utkarshp1161/thickness-mapping-webapp

AI.tldr: A tunable human-AI-assisted workflow framework for automated STEM image analysis in semiconductor metrology, specifically for layer thickness and interface roughness quantification in multilayer thin films.
AI.motivation: Current semiconductor metrology faces a trade-off between rigid automated pipelines that are brittle to sample variability and purely manual approaches that are slow and subjective. There is a need for flexible yet scalable analysis methods.
AI.method: The framework integrates gradient-based peak detection with interactive correction modules, allowing human input at the design stage while maintaining fully automated execution across samples. Implemented as a web-based interface processing TEM/EMD files directly with noise reduction and interface tracking algorithms.
AI.result: The system outputs statistical roughness and thickness metrics with nanometer precision for multilayer thin films.
AI.conclusion: This architecture provides a general approach toward adaptive, reusable metrology workflows, bridging human insight and machine precision for scalable, standardized analysis in semiconductor manufacturing.

title: Improving YBa$_2$Cu$_3$O$_{7-δ}$ annealing times through a combining-temperatures route
summary: The oxygenation process at constant temperature of YBa$_2$Cu$_3$O$_{7-δ}$ (YBCO) was systematically investigated in the temperature range from 300 $^o$C to 800 $^o$C. With this purpose, fully deoxygenated powder samples was exposed to an oxygen saturated atmosphere, and the evolution of their mass was recorded as a function of time using a thermogravimetric balance. Results reveal a strong dependence of both: the oxygenation kinetics and the final oxygen saturation level, on the temperature used for oxygenation. Moreover, results show that higher oxygen temperatures promote faster oxygen absorption but lead to lower saturation levels (higher final $δ$ values), whereas lower oxygen temperatures result in slower kinetics but enable the system to approach better oxygenation conditions in order to improve the final superconducting properties of the material. In addition to our measurements, a comparative analysis between oxygenation levels at the oxygen temperatures under study was performed in the range around oxygen saturation ($δ$ $<$ 0.3). Consequently, an oxygenation protocol based on a combination of several oxygenation temperatures is proposed. As a first approach, results from a protocol with just two different oxygenation temperatures is compared with results coming from using just one oxygenation temperature. Outstandingly, a protocol with a first oxygenation step at high temperatures and a second one at low temperatures demonstrates to improve oxygenation times in near a 30 \% for reaching $δ$ values below 0.1 and in near a 60 \% for reaching $δ$ values around 0.12. Finally, we trust that our results are of direct application on industry since size of grain used herein are in scales of typical thickness of superconductor tapes.

AI.tldr: Systematic investigation of YBCO oxygenation kinetics between 300-800°C reveals temperature-dependent behavior. Higher temperatures yield faster but incomplete oxygenation, while lower temperatures are slower but achieve better saturation. A two-temperature protocol reduces oxygenation time by 30-60%, with direct industrial applicability.
AI.motivation: The study addresses the oxygenation process of YBa2Cu3O7-δ (YBCO) superconductor, which is critical for optimizing its superconducting properties. The motivation is to understand how temperature affects oxygenation kinetics and final oxygen content, and to develop an efficient oxygenation protocol for industrial applications.
AI.method: Fully deoxygenated YBCO powder samples were exposed to oxygen-saturated atmosphere at constant temperatures ranging from 300°C to 800°C. The evolution of mass was recorded as a function of time using thermogravimetric analysis. A comparative analysis was performed around oxygen saturation (δ < 0.3), and a two-temperature oxygenation protocol was proposed and tested.
AI.result: Higher oxygenation temperatures promote faster oxygen absorption but lead to lower saturation levels (higher δ values), while lower temperatures result in slower kinetics but better oxygenation conditions. The two-step protocol (high temperature first, then low temperature) improves oxygenation times by ~30% for δ < 0.1 and ~60% for δ ≈ 0.12.
AI.conclusion: A combined temperature oxygenation protocol significantly improves oxygenation efficiency compared to single-temperature processes. This approach is directly applicable to industrial superconductor tape manufacturing due to the grain size used in the study.

title: Comparative study of room temperature and quench condensed bismuth films: morphology and electronic characteristics
summary: A comparison between properties of bismuth thin films deposited at substrate temperatures of 296 K (room temperature) and 77 K (quench condensed) is studied across epitaxial, amorphous, and van der Waals substrates. The experiments demonstrate changes in crystallinity, morphology, and electrical transport arising from the influence of substrate temperature. Moreover, the work highlights changes in grain size, roughness, X-ray diffraction peak intensities, and preferred orientation between the two deposition temperatures. The orientation of the films deposited at 77 K is preferentially (110), compared to (111) for films deposited at room temperature. Films grown at 77 K differ from those deposited at room temperature, exhibiting lower surface roughness but smaller grain size, which leads to increased electrical resistivity in quench condensed films. The decrease of substrate temperature during the deposition appears to induce slightly more strain in depositions on the amorphous and van der Waals substrates than on the epitaxial substrates. Lastly, quench condensed films exhibit lower carrier mobility and lower carrier density compared to room temperature films. This study elucidates previously incompletely understood processes in bismuth deposition and raises new questions regarding growth on van der Waals surfaces.

AI.tldr: This study compares bismuth thin films deposited at room temperature (296K) and cryogenic temperature (77K) across epitaxial, amorphous, and van der Waals substrates. Lower deposition temperature leads to (110) orientation instead of (111), smaller grains, lower roughness, higher resistivity, and reduced carrier mobility/density, with greater strain on amorphous and van der Waals substrates.
AI.motivation: To understand how substrate temperature during deposition affects the crystallinity, morphology, and electrical properties of bismuth thin films, particularly on different substrate types including van der Waals surfaces.
AI.method: Bismuth thin films were deposited at two substrate temperatures (296K and 77K) across epitaxial, amorphous, and van der Waals substrates. The films were characterized using X-ray diffraction, morphology analysis, and electrical transport measurements.
AI.result: Films deposited at 77K exhibit (110) preferred orientation versus (111) for room temperature films. Quench condensed films show lower surface roughness but smaller grain size, resulting in higher electrical resistivity. They also demonstrate lower carrier mobility and density. More strain is induced on amorphous and van der Waals substrates compared to epitaxial substrates.
AI.conclusion: This study provides insights into how substrate temperature affects bismuth thin film growth across different substrate types, revealing that quench condensation leads to distinct structural and electronic properties. The findings highlight the complexity of film growth on van der Waals surfaces and suggest further investigation is needed to fully understand these processes.

title: Magnetoelectric Control of Toroidal Moment in Ferroaxial Crystal PbMn$_{2}$Ni$_{6}$Te$_{3}$O$_{18}$
summary: Ferroic multipole orders break some symmetry and often activate unique physical properties, such as nonreciprocal directional dichroism and linear magnetoelectric effects. Here, we demonstrate the control of magnetic toroidal orientation in PbMn$_{2}$Ni$_{6}$Te$_{3}$O$_{18}$ through the application of electric and magnetic fields in two distinct configurations. Through directional dichroism, we successfully visualize magnetic ferrotoroidic domains, establishing the intrinsic coupling among magnetic toroidal moment, crystallographic ferroaxial moment, and magnetoelectric monopoles. Our findings not only present an effective pathway for controlling magnetic toroidal moment but also provide a novel approach for investigating ferroaxial ordering.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates control of magnetic toroidal moment in PbMn₂Ni₆Te₃O₁₈ through electric and magnetic fields, and visualizes magnetic ferrotoroidic domains using directional dichroism.
AI.motivation: Ferroic multipole orders can activate unique physical properties like nonreciprocal directional dichroism and linear magnetoelectric effects, but controlling magnetic toroidal orientation and understanding its coupling with other orders remains challenging.
AI.method: The researchers applied electric and magnetic fields in two distinct configurations to control magnetic toroidal orientation in PbMn₂Ni₆Te₃O₁₈, and used directional dichroism to visualize magnetic ferrotoroidic domains.
AI.result: They successfully controlled magnetic toroidal orientation and established intrinsic coupling among magnetic toroidal moment, crystallographic ferroaxial moment, and magnetoelectric monopoles.
AI.conclusion: This work provides an effective pathway for controlling magnetic toroidal moment and offers a novel approach for investigating ferroaxial ordering.

title: Emergent superconductivity at 16.3 K in an altermagnetic candidate Na$_{2-x}$V$_2$Se$_2$O with broken inversion symmetry
summary: Altermagnets (AMs), characterized by zero net magnetization and momentum-dependent spin splitting, are anticipated to hold significant potential for generating multiple exotic and uncommon superconducting states. However, superconductivity has not yet been realized in AMs to date. Recently, two-dimensional (2D) V$_2$Ch$_2$O (Ch = Se, Te) monolayers, as well as AV$_2$Ch$_2$O (A = K, Rb, Cs) crystals containing [V$_2$Ch$_2$O]$^{δ-}$ building layers, have been predicted and/or demonstrated to be promising altermagnetic materials. Our preliminary attempts to explore superconductivity in these materials by applying pressure or chemical doping were unsuccessful. Here we report the discovery of superconductivity at a relatively high transition temperature of ~ 16.3 K in a newly synthesized layered compound, Na$_{2-x}$V$_2$Se$_2$O, a variant of AV$_2$Ch$_2$O. In this structure, the [V$_2$Ch$_2$O]$^{δ-}$ layers are interspersed with double layers of Na$^+$ instead of a single layer of A$^+$, with sodium sites being only half-filled. This new family of layered vanadium oxychalcogenides, lacking inversion symmetry, represents an intriguing platform for exploring altermagnetic superconductors, and holds the potential to reveal novel phenomena, such as topological states, van Hove singularities, and finite-momentum superconductivity. Furthermore, this material acts as a "bridge" between the cuprate/nickelate and iron-pnictide high temperature superconductors, providing new hope and opportunity to expand the category of layered superconductors with higher critical temperatures (T$_c$) and enhancing our understanding of the underlying mechanisms in these systems.

AI.tldr: Discovery of superconductivity at 16.3 K in Na₂₋ₓV₂Se₂O, a new layered vanadium oxychalcogenide altermagnetic material, representing the first observed superconductivity in altermagnets and serving as a bridge between different families of high-Tc superconductors.
AI.motivation: Altermagnets (AMs) have zero net magnetization and momentum-dependent spin splitting, making them promising candidates for exotic superconducting states. However, superconductivity had never been realized in AMs despite attempts via pressure or chemical doping on related materials (V₂Ch₂O monolayers and AV₂Ch₂O crystals).
AI.method: Synthesis of a new layered compound Na₂₋ₓV₂Se₂O, a variant of AV₂Ch₂O where [V₂Ch₂O]δ- layers are interspersed with double layers of Na+ instead of single A+ layers, with sodium sites only half-filled.
AI.result: Successfully discovered superconductivity at relatively high transition temperature of ~16.3 K in this new altermagnetic material - the first observation of superconductivity in altermagnets.
AI.conclusion: This material represents an intriguing platform for exploring altermagnetic superconductors with potential for topological states, van Hove singularities, and finite-momentum superconductivity. It bridges cuprate/nickelate and iron-pnictide high-temperature superconductors, offering new opportunities to expand layered superconductor categories with higher Tc and enhance understanding of superconducting mechanisms.

title: Robust $d$-wave altermagnetism in $\mathrm{RbCr_2Se_2O}$
summary: The $\mathrm{KV_2Se_2O}$, $\mathrm{Rb_{1-δ}V_2Te_2O}$ and $\mathrm{Cs_{1-δ}V_2Te_2O}$ are experimentally confirmed to adopt either C-type or G-type antiferromagnetic configuration, corresponding to apparent or hidden altermagnetism. However, their nearly degenerate energies lead to inconsistent experimental assignments between the two antiferromagnetic configurations. Here, we predict that the experimentally synthesized $\mathrm{RbCr_2Se_2O}$ is a robust $d$-wave altermagnetic metal, since the energy difference between C-type and G-type configurations is large, which is independent of electron correlation strength and van der Waals interaction. Upon applying in-plane uniaxial strain, $\mathrm{RbCr_2Se_2O}$ can generate a net total magnetic moment via a direct piezomagnetic effect, which is distinct from semiconductor that typically requires carrier doping in addition to strain. This provides an experimental strategy for distinguishing the G-type antiferromagnetic configuration, in which the total magnetic moment remains zero under uniaxial strain. Our work presents an isostructural $d$-wave altermagnetic $\mathrm{RbCr_2Se_2O}$ analogous to $\mathrm{KV_2Se_2O}$, $\mathrm{Rb_{1-δ}V_2Te_2O}$ and $\mathrm{Cs_{1-δ}V_2Te_2O}$, which can facilitate further experimental verification. Furthermore, these results are universal across materials of this family $\mathrm{XCr_2Y_2O}$ (X=K, Rb, Cs; Y=Se, Te), thus expanding the family of altermagnets.

AI.tldr: The paper predicts RbCr2Se2O as a robust d-wave altermagnetic metal with a large energy difference between C-type and G-type antiferromagnetic configurations, distinct from other materials in the family which have nearly degenerate energies. It proposes using in-plane uniaxial strain to generate a net magnetic moment via a piezomagnetic effect to distinguish the G-type antiferromagnetic configuration.
AI.motivation: The existing materials like KV2Se2O, Rb1-δV2Te2O, and Cs1-δV2Te2O exhibit either C-type or G-type antiferromagnetic configurations corresponding to apparent or hidden altermagnetism, but their nearly degenerate energies lead to inconsistent experimental assignments. The need is to find a robust altermagnetic material and an experimental method to distinguish between these configurations.
AI.method: The study likely employs first-principles calculations (e.g., density functional theory) to predict the magnetic configurations and energy differences, though the abstract does not explicitly specify the computational method.
AI.result: RbCr2Se2O is predicted to be a robust d-wave altermagnetic metal with a large energy difference between C-type and G-type configurations, independent of electron correlation strength and van der Waals interaction. Under in-plane uniaxial strain, it can generate a net total magnetic moment via a piezomagnetic effect, unlike semiconductors that require carrier doping. This effect allows distinguishing the G-type antiferromagnetic configuration, where the total magnetic moment remains zero under strain. The results are universal across the XCr2Y2O family (X=K, Rb, Cs; Y=Se, Te).
AI.conclusion: The discovery of RbCr2Se2O as a robust d-wave altermagnet expands the family of altermagnets and provides an experimental strategy to resolve the ambiguity in antiferromagnetic configurations through piezomagnetic effects under uniaxial strain.

title: The $\mathbb{Z}_N^{\times 3}$ symmetry protected boundary modes in two-dimensional Potts paramagnets
summary: We construct and analyze a class of one-dimensional boundary Hamiltonians arising from two-dimensional symmetry-protected topological phases with $\mathbb{Z}_N^{\times 3}$ symmetry on a triangular lattice. Using a cohomology-based transformation, the lattice models for the edge modes are explicitly obtained, and their structure is shown to be governed by the arithmetic properties of $N$. For prime $N$, the boundary theory admits a formulation in terms of mutually commuting Temperley-Lieb algebras. For the composite values of $N$, the models exhibit hierarchical or factorized structures. We demonstrate that all phases can be understood in terms of primary models augmented by local defect degrees of freedom that partition the system into independent segments. Finally, the global symmetry is realized on the boundary in a non-on-site and anomalous manner via a projective representation, directly realizing the corresponding 't Hooft anomaly.

AI.tldr: This paper constructs and analyzes 1D boundary Hamiltonians from 2D SPT phases with ℤ_N^×3 symmetry on a triangular lattice. Using cohomology, they show prime N yields commuting Temperley-Lieb algebras while composite N gives hierarchical structures. The boundary symmetry acts anomalously via projective representation, realizing 't Hooft anomaly.
AI.motivation: The paper seeks to understand how symmetry-protected topological (SPT) phases in two dimensions manifest on their boundaries, specifically for a system with ℤ_N^×3 symmetry on a triangular lattice. The goal is to explicitly construct boundary Hamiltonians and understand how the global symmetry acts anomalously (non-on-site), which relates to the 't Hooft anomaly matching condition.
AI.method: The authors use a cohomology-based transformation to derive lattice models for the edge modes. They analyze the arithmetic properties of N (whether prime or composite) to reveal the structure of the boundary theories. The analysis involves Temperley-Lieb algebras and studies projective representations.
AI.result: For prime N, the boundary theory admits a formulation in terms of mutually commuting Temperley-Lieb algebras. For composite N, the models exhibit hierarchical or factorized structures. All phases can be understood as primary models augmented by local defect degrees of freedom that partition the system into independent segments. The global symmetry is realized non-on-site via a projective representation, directly realizing the 't Hooft anomaly.
AI.conclusion: The boundary physics of 2D SPT phases with ℤ_N^×3 symmetry is governed by the arithmetic of N. Prime N gives Temperley-Lieb algebraic structure, while composite N leads to hierarchical/factorized models. The anomalous boundary symmetry provides a direct realization of 't Hooft anomaly matching.

title: Detecting pairing symmetry of bilayer nickelates using electronic Raman scattering
summary: The recent discovery of high-temperature superconductivity in both bulk and thin-film bilayer nickelates La$_3$Ni$_2$O$_7$ has garnered significant attention. However, the corresponding pairing symmetry remains debated in both experiments and theoretical studies due to conflicting experimental evidence from bulk and thin-film materials. In this work, we examine the electronic Raman response across different channels for various pairing symmetries within a two-orbital bilayer model. By comparing Raman susceptibilities obtained from multiorbital and band-additive approaches, we demonstrate that Raman response can distinguish between different pairing symmetries and identify pocket-dependent gap amplitudes for both fully gapped and nodal superconducting states. Specifically, the nodal $d_{x^2-y^2}/d_{xy}$-wave pairing exhibits robust low-energy power-law behavior, distinct from a fully gapped pairing. Additionally, for the $s_{\pm}$-wave pairing, the detailed gap anisotropy on the $β$ pocket can be determined. Possible experimental implications are also discussed. Our results highlight the crucial role of multiorbital effects in shaping the Raman spectra and establish electronic Raman scattering as a powerful and symmetry-resolved probe for determining the superconducting gap in unconventional superconductors.

AI.tldr: This study examines electronic Raman response across different pairing symmetries in bilayer nickelates La3Ni2O7 using a two-orbital bilayer model, demonstrating that Raman spectroscopy can distinguish between fully gapped and nodal superconducting states and identify pocket-dependent gap amplitudes.
AI.motivation: The pairing symmetry in high-Tc superconductivity of bilayer nickelates remains debated due to conflicting experimental evidence from bulk and thin-film materials. A reliable probe to determine the superconducting gap symmetry is urgently needed.
AI.method: The authors used a two-orbital bilayer model and compared Raman susceptibilities from multiorbital and band-additive approaches to analyze different pairing symmetries including nodal d-wave and s±-wave.
AI.result: The nodal d_{x^2-y^2}/d_{xy}-wave pairing exhibits robust low-energy power-law behavior distinct from fully gapped pairing. For s±-wave pairing, gap anisotropy on the β pocket can be determined. Multiorbital effects are crucial in shaping Raman spectra.
AI.conclusion: Electronic Raman scattering is established as a powerful symmetry-resolved probe for determining superconducting gap symmetry in unconventional superconductors, highlighting the importance of multiorbital effects in interpreting Raman spectra.

title: Bipolar plates for the next generation of proton exchange membrane fuel cells (PEMFCs): A review of the latest processing methods for unconventional flow channels
summary: The rapid, unsustainable depletion of finite fossil fuel resources and their environmental consequences demand the deployment of affordable clean and sustainable energy solutions. Polymer electrolyte membrane fuel cell (PEMFC) technology is an important pathway in decarbonization of modern energy systems, especially when fueled by high-purity green hydrogen. In PEMFCs, bipolar plates largely determine cell efficiency, longevity, and affordability, which in turn depends on both material selection and design of the embedded flow channels. Conventional manufacturing processes have long been used to fabricate standard bipolar plate designs; however, they are incompatible with unconventional, intricate geometries due to their insufficient resolution and precision in fabrication of fine features, and reliance on multi-step post-processing modifications that limit their design adaptability. This lack of design flexibility impedes the translation of innovative laboratory-scale concepts to industrial-scale production and their practical adoption. In recent years, a growing body of research publications and patent disclosures has reported advanced manufacturing methods, such as additive manufacturing, capable of producing intricate bipolar plate geometries at competitive costs. However, a discussion of these manufacturing approaches, along with an assessment of their scalability and industrial readiness, remains absent in the literature. This study aims to fill this gap. It outlines recent progress and proposes future research directions toward affordable and efficient bipolar plate solutions for advanced PEMFC systems.

AI.tldr: This paper reviews advanced manufacturing methods for bipolar plates in PEM fuel cells, addressing the gap in literature regarding scalability and industrial readiness of approaches like additive manufacturing.
AI.motivation: The rapid depletion of fossil fuels and environmental concerns demand clean energy solutions. PEMFCs are key for decarbonization, but bipolar plates—critical for efficiency, longevity, and affordability—face manufacturing limitations. Conventional processes cannot produce intricate geometries needed for innovative designs, hindering translation from lab to industrial scale.
AI.method: This study outlines recent progress in advanced manufacturing methods (e.g., additive manufacturing) for producing intricate bipolar plate geometries and assesses their scalability and industrial readiness.
AI.result: The paper presents a comprehensive review of manufacturing approaches for bipolar plates and proposes future research directions toward affordable and efficient solutions for advanced PEMFC systems.
AI.conclusion: This work addresses the missing discussion in literature about manufacturing approaches for bipolar plates, providing insights into scalable and industrially ready solutions to advance PEMFC technology.

title: Excitations across the equilibrium and photoinduced `hidden' states of magnetoresistive manganites
summary: "Hidden" phases, generated using ultrafast laser pulses (few hundred femtoseconds), with properties distinct from thermodynamic equilibrium, are appealing for technologies because they can be long-lived, with lifetimes of hours or weeks, and reversible with temperature sweeping or extra pulses. In this regard, La$_{2/3}$Ca$_{1/3}$MnO$_3$ (LCMO) stands out due to its tunability through epitaxial strain, which can drive the bulk ferromagnetic metal (FMM) into an antiferromagnetic insulator (AFI), and its susceptibility to photo-induced transitions. Indeed, AFI LCMO displays a long-lived photo-induced transition into a putative 'hidden' phase whose exact nature and excitations are still largely unknown. Here, we combine ultrafast photo-excitation in the near infrared with in situ transport, x-ray absorption (XAS), and Resonant Inelastic X-ray Scattering (RIXS) to investigate the excitations (polarons, phonons, and orbital) of the photo-excited phase of LCMO and contrast them with the thermodynamic phases achieved through strain and temperature. In the thermodynamic regime, we establish the correlation between polarons and transport, placing them in the 'strong coupling' regime of the Holstein model. Upon photo-excitation of LCMO-AFI, we uncover a long-lived phase characterized by the softening of the polaron excitations, the partial suppression of the Jahn-Teller distortion, and nearly unchanged phonons, showing the emergence of a photo-excited state absent in the equilibrium phase diagram. Finally, by varying temperature, epitaxial strain, and photo-excitation fluence, we construct a polaron phase diagram and identify the key spectroscopic signatures of each phase. Our laser-RIXS approach establishes a versatile platform for exploring photo-induced 'hidden' phases in quantum materials in non-stroboscopic conditions.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates photo-induced "hidden" phases in La2/3Ca1/3MnO3 (LCMO) using ultrafast laser pulses combined with in situ transport, X-ray absorption, and Resonant Inelastic X-ray Scattering (RIXS). The study reveals a long-lived photo-excited phase with softened polarons, suppressed Jahn-Teller distortion, and constructs a polaron phase diagram mapping the key spectroscopic signatures across different conditions.
AI.motivation: The research addresses the need to understand hidden phases in quantum materials—non-equilibrium states induced by ultrafast laser pulses that can be long-lived (hours/weeks) and reversible. LCMO is particularly interesting due to its tunability via epitaxial strain (switching between ferromagnetic metal and antiferromagnetic insulator) and susceptibility to photo-induced transitions. The exact nature of the photo-induced hidden phase in LCMO was previously unknown.
AI.method: The study combined ultrafast photo-excitation in the near-infrared with three in situ techniques: transport measurements, X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS), and Resonant Inelastic X-ray Scattering (RIXS). Parameters were varied including temperature, epitaxial strain, and photo-excitation fluence to systematically map the phase behavior.
AI.result: In the thermodynamic regime, polarons were placed in the 'strong coupling' regime of the Holstein model. Upon photo-excitation of LCMO-AFI, a long-lived phase was uncovered characterized by: (1) softening of polaron excitations, (2) partial suppression of the Jahn-Teller distortion, and (3) nearly unchanged phonons. This represents a photo-excited state absent in the equilibrium phase diagram. A polaron phase diagram was constructed identifying key spectroscopic signatures of each phase.
AI.conclusion: The laser-RIXS approach establishes a versatile platform for exploring photo-induced hidden phases in quantum materials under non-stroboscopic conditions. This methodology enables the discovery of new photo-excited states that do not exist in equilibrium, opening pathways for understanding and controlling hidden phases in complex materials.

title: Uniaxial Compression-Induced Anisotropy and Electronic Dimensionality in the Iron-Based Superconductor FeSe
summary: The evolution of the superconducting transition temperature ($T_c$) in FeSe was investigated under in-plane, out-of-plane, and hydrostatic compression. For pressures up to 0.6 GPa, $T_c$ increases regardless of the compression mode, consistent with the suppression of nematic ordering. However, once nematicity is suppressed, $T_c$ exhibits a striking directional dependence: out-of-plane compression shows behavior similar to the hydrostatic case, with a sharp increase in $T_c$, whereas in-plane compression suppresses superconductivity. First-principles calculations suggest that in-plane compression shifts a hybridized band of Se $p_z$ and Fe $d_{x^2-y^2}$ character so that it crosses the Fermi level along the $Γ$-Z direction, leading to the emergence of an additional metallic band. This leads to an increased three-dimensionality of the electronic structure and may be interpreted as a possible Lifshitz-type change in the Fermi surface. These results indicate that the dimensionality of the electronic structure plays a key role in determining the $T_c$ response of FeSe under different compression modes.

AI.tldr: Study investigating superconducting transition temperature (Tc) in FeSe under different compression modes (in-plane, out-of-plane, hydrostatic) up to 0.6 GPa, revealing directional dependence after nematicity suppression.
AI.motivation: Understand how dimensionality of electronic structure affects Tc response in FeSe under different compression directions.
AI.method: Combined experimental (in-plane, out-of-plane, hydrostatic compression) and first-principles calculations to study pressure effects on FeSe.
AI.result: Tc increases under all compression modes up to 0.6 GPa due to nematic ordering suppression. After nematicity suppression: out-of-plane and hydrostatic show sharp Tc increase; in-plane suppresses superconductivity. Calculations show in-plane compression shifts hybridized Se pz - Fe dx2-y2 band crossing Fermi level along Γ-Z direction, increasing 3D electronic structure.
AI.conclusion: Electronic structure dimensionality plays a key role in determining Tc response of FeSe under different compression modes. Possible Lifshitz-type Fermi surface change observed.

title: Radio-Frequency-Driven Reshaping of the Mesoscale Charge-Density-Wave Landscape in 1T-TaS2 Thin-Film Devices
summary: Radio-frequency excitation directly reshapes the mesoscale charge-density-wave landscape in quasi-two-dimensional 1T-TaS2 thin films. Under combined RF and DC bias, the hysteretic current-voltage characteristics associated with the nearly commensurate-incommensurate transition are strongly altered, displaying RF-driven collapse, branching, and multiple step-like features that depend on frequency and drive amplitude. In-situ Raman measurements show enhanced intensity and linewidth narrowing of low-frequency CDW phonon modes, consistent with reduced dephasing and increased coherence of the periodic lattice distortion under RF drive. This behavior is captured by combining an overdamped time-dependent Ginzburg-Landau description of the commensurate CDW with a morphology-informed percolative resistor-capacitor transport model. The simulations indicate that oscillatory driving anneals frustrated domain configurations, reduces domain-wall density, and reorganizes the discommensuration network, while the transport model reproduces the resulting hysteresis, avalanche-like pathways, and RF-induced conductance steps. RF driving therefore provides an effective route for controlling collective electron-phonon order and accessing metastable transport states in 1T-TaS2, with implications for reconfigurable RF electronics, memory, and unconventional computing based on correlated materials.

AI.tldr: This study demonstrates that radio-frequency excitation can effectively control charge-density-wave order in 1T-TaS2 thin films, altering hysteresis characteristics and enhancing phonon coherence. The findings, supported by Raman spectroscopy and combined Ginzburg-Landau/RC transport modeling, suggest promising applications in reconfigurable electronics and memory devices based on correlated materials.
AI.motivation: To explore how radio-frequency excitation can be used to control collective electron-phonon order in correlated materials, specifically to understand and manipulate charge-density-wave (CDW) states in 1T-TaS2 thin films.
AI.method: Combined RF and DC bias experiments on quasi-two-dimensional 1T-TaS2 thin films, in-situ Raman spectroscopy measurements, and theoretical modeling using an overdamped time-dependent Ginzburg-Landau description combined with a morphology-informed percolative resistor-capacitor transport model.
AI.result: RF excitation directly reshapes the mesoscale CDW landscape, causing hysteretic current-voltage characteristics to display RF-driven collapse, branching, and multiple step-like features depending on frequency and drive amplitude. Raman measurements show enhanced intensity and linewidth narrowing of low-frequency CDW phonon modes, indicating reduced dephasing and increased coherence. Simulations reveal that oscillatory driving anneals frustrated domain configurations, reduces domain-wall density, and reorganizes the discommensuration network.
AI.conclusion: RF driving provides an effective route for controlling collective electron-phonon order and accessing metastable transport states in 1T-TaS2. This has implications for reconfigurable RF electronics, memory, and unconventional computing based on correlated materials.

title: The multichannel Dyson equation for double ionisation spectroscopies
summary: Several photoemission spectroscopies and, in particular, Auger spectroscopy, involve double-ionization processes. For the numerical simulation of these spectroscopies it is convenient to use the particle-particle channel of the two-body Green's functions since its poles correspond to excitation energies in which the final state has two more particles (holes or electrons) than the initial state. In standard approaches it is approximated within the random phase approximation. As a consequence only the quasiparticles of the photoemission spectrum are captured but none of the satellites features. In this work, we go beyond this approximation by employing the multichannel Dyson equation. By coupling the particle-particle two-body Green's function to the 3-hole-1-electron and 3-electron-1-hole channels of the four-body Green's function, the multichannel Dyson equation incorporates correlations beyond the RPA in a straightforward way. We are thus able to describe both quasiparticles and satellites in the photoemission spectra.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a method to simulate photoemission spectroscopies involving double-ionization processes by using the multichannel Dyson equation, which extends beyond the standard random phase approximation to capture both quasiparticle and satellite features in the spectra.
AI.motivation: Standard random phase approximation (RPA) approaches to photoemission spectroscopy only capture quasiparticle features but miss satellite features. The limitation arises because RPA only addresses single-particle excitations and cannot describe the satellite structures that arise from more complex correlation effects in double-ionization processes.
AI.method: The authors employ the multichannel Dyson equation approach, coupling the particle-particle two-body Green's function to the 3-hole-1-electron and 3-electron-1-hole channels of the four-body Green's function. This allows incorporation of correlations beyond the random phase approximation.
AI.result: By going beyond RPA through the multichannel Dyson equation, the authors demonstrate the ability to describe both quasiparticles and satellites in photoemission spectra, providing a more complete characterization of the excitation spectrum.
AI.conclusion: This work advances the theoretical description of photoemission spectroscopies by capturing both quasiparticles and satellite features, which was previously not possible within standard RPA-based approaches. The multichannel Dyson equation framework provides a more complete picture of electron correlation effects in double-ionization processes.

title: Electronic structure and correlation of La$_4$Co$_2$NiO$_8$Cl$_2$: a theoretical proposal for a La$_4$Ni$_3$O$_{10}$-like high-temperature superconductor
summary: Based on the discovery of high-temperature superconductivity in the bilayer nickelate La$_3$Ni$_2$O$_7$, several Co-based La$_3$Ni$_2$O$_7$-like materials were theoretically predicted as possible high-temperature superconductors by electron doping. Motivated by these findings and the subsequent discovery of superconductivity in the trilayer nickelate La$_4$Ni$_3$O$_{10}$ under high pressure, we propose and investigate a Co-based La$_4$Ni$_3$O$_{10}$-like material. With electron doping to the high-pressure trilayer cobaltate La$_4$Co$_3$O$_{10}$, using density functional theory combined with dynamical mean-field theory (DFT+DMFT), we find that the resulting compound La$_4$Co$_2$NiO$_8$Cl$_2$ exhibits a crystal structure and a strongly correlated electronic structure similar to those of La$_4$Ni$_3$O$_{10}$ under high pressure. This suggests that this new compound may host high-temperature superconductivity.

AI.tldr: This paper predicts a new cobalt-based material La4Co2NiO8Cl2 that may exhibit high-temperature superconductivity, based on computational studies connecting it to the recently discovered superconducting nickelate La4Ni3O10 under high pressure.
AI.motivation: Following the discovery of high-temperature superconductivity in bilayer nickelate La3Ni2O7 and subsequent findings in trilayer nickelate La4Ni3O10 under high pressure, the authors seek to explore Co-based analogues as potential high-temperature superconductors.
AI.method: The authors used density functional theory combined with dynamical mean-field theory (DFT+DMFT) to investigate electron-doped La4Co3O10 (a high-pressure trilayer cobaltate), searching for compounds with similar structural and electronic properties to superconducting nickelates.
AI.result: The calculated compound La4Co2NiO8Cl2 exhibits a crystal structure and strongly correlated electronic structure similar to La4Ni3O10 under high pressure.
AI.conclusion: The new compound La4Co2NiO8Cl2 is predicted to potentially host high-temperature superconductivity, expanding the search for superconducting materials beyond nickelates to cobalt-based systems.

title: Magnetoelastic instabilities in kagome antiferromagnet Mn3-xGa
summary: We present a systematic study of the structural, magnetic, and transport properties of hexagonal Mn3-xGa alloys, revealing a series of composition-controlled emergent phenomena. By tuning the Mn concentration, we uncover distinct lattice responses, including a zero thermal expansion-like volume compensation behavior in Mn-poor compositions and a magnetoelastic-driven, field-assisted structural phase transition in Mn-rich samples. These lattice instabilities are accompanied by correlated magnetic and transport anomalies, including metamagnetic transitions, negative magnetoresistance, and anomalous Hall sign reversal. First-principles calculations demonstrate that the Hall sign reversal originates from crystal-symmetry breaking rather than magnetic reorientation alone. Our results establish composition as the key control parameter governing magnetoelastic coupling in Mn3-xGa, providing a unified framework to tailor structural, magnetic, and topological transport properties in kagome antiferromagnets and reconcile previously disparate experimental observations.

AI.tldr: Systematic study of hexagonal Mn3-xGa alloys revealing composition-controlled structural, magnetic, and topological transport properties, including zero thermal expansion behavior, field-assisted structural transitions, and anomalous Hall sign reversal.
AI.motivation: Kagome antiferromagnets like Mn3-xGa exhibit complex magnetoelastic coupling, but the relationship between composition and emergent phenomena (structural transitions, magnetic anomalies, topological transport) remains poorly understood, limiting property engineering.
AI.method: Systematic composition tuning of Mn concentration in Mn3-xGa; combined experimental measurements (structural, magnetic, transport properties) and first-principles calculations to investigate lattice responses and Hall effect origins.
AI.result: Discovery of composition-dependent phenomena: (1) Zero thermal expansion-like volume compensation in Mn-poor samples; (2) Magnetoelastic-driven field-assisted structural phase transition in Mn-rich samples; (3) Correlated magnetic/transport anomalies including metamagnetic transitions, negative magnetoresistance, and anomalous Hall sign reversal; (4) Hall sign reversal originates from crystal-symmetry breaking rather than magnetic reorientation.
AI.conclusion: Composition is the key control parameter governing magnetoelastic coupling in Mn3-xGa; provides unified framework for tailoring structural, magnetic, and topological transport properties in kagome antiferromagnets and reconciling disparate experimental observations.

title: Revealing buried ferroelectric topologies by depth-resolved electron diffraction imaging
comment: 33 pages, 4 figures, 12 supplementary figures, 1 supplementary table
summary: Nanoscale topological polar textures promise new functionalities for ferroelectric memories and logic, yet their three-dimensional structure and mesoscale organization remain experimentally inaccessible. Here we introduce depth-resolved electron diffraction imaging (DREDI), a fast, non-destructive, method that maps polarization with <50 nm lateral and <10 nm depth sensitivity within fraction of a second. Its high acquisition speed enables the first continuous polarization mapping across six orders of magnitude in length scale, from nanometers to millimeters. Using epitaxial BiFeO3 films, DREDI reveals a hidden depth evolution of polar textures: surface 71-degree stripes evolve into subsurface flux-closure vortices that bifurcate into three-fold vertices near the bottom interface. Cross-sectional multi-slice electron ptychography and phase-field modeling confirm these buried configurations and attribute them to strain heterogeneity and ferroelastic twinning in the SrRuO3 electrode. Large-area analysis further shows that vertex-like frustration forms a mesoscale percolating network above a critical length scale of 4 um. DREDI enables real-time, volumetric studies of buried topological textures in ferroic nanomaterials.

AI.tldr: New electron diffraction imaging technique (DREDI) enables first continuous polarization mapping across six orders of magnitude (nm to mm), revealing hidden 3D polar texture evolution in ferroelectric BiFeO3 films from surface stripes to subsurface vortices to three-fold vertices, controlled by strain and ferroelastic twinning in the electrode.
AI.motivation: Nanoscale topological polar textures in ferroelectrics promise new functionalities for ferroelectric memories and logic, but their three-dimensional structure and mesoscale organization remained experimentally inaccessible with existing techniques.
AI.method: Depth-resolved electron diffraction imaging (DREDI) - a fast, non-destructive method mapping polarization with <50 nm lateral and <10 nm depth sensitivity within fractions of a second. The method was validated using cross-sectional multi-slice electron ptychography and phase-field modeling.
AI.result: DREDI revealed a hidden depth evolution of polar textures in epitaxial BiFeO3 films: surface 71-degree stripes evolve into subsurface flux-closure vortices that bifurcate into three-fold vertices near the bottom interface. The configurations are attributed to strain heterogeneity and ferroelastic twinning in the SrRuO3 electrode. Large-area analysis showed that vertex-like frustration forms a mesoscale percolating network above a critical length scale of 4 μm.
AI.conclusion: DREDI represents a breakthrough in characterizing buried topological textures in ferroic nanomaterials, enabling real-time, volumetric studies that were previously impossible. The technique reveals hidden depth evolution of polar structures and demonstrates that vertex-like frustration forms mesoscale percolating networks above a critical length scale of 4 μm, opening new avenues for understanding and engineering ferroelectric devices.

title: Beyond Beryllium: AI-Accelerated Materials Discovery for Interstellar Spacecraft Shielding
summary: Project Daedalus (1973--1978), the most detailed interstellar probe design study ever conducted, specified a 9 mm beryllium erosion shield to protect the spacecraft payload during its 5.9 light-year cruise to Barnard's Star at 12% of the speed of light. This design, however, predated both the isolation of two-dimensional materials and the development of graph neural network (GNN) property predictors. Here, we systematically screen 20 candidate materials--spanning conventional aerospace metals, transition metal dichalcogenides, and ultra-high-temperature ceramics--using density functional theory (DFT) data from the JARVIS database (76,000 materials) with independent validation by the Atomistic Line Graph Neural Network (ALIGNN). We evaluate candidates across four criteria: specific mechanical stiffness (KV/rho), sputtering resistance, thermal neutron absorption cross-section, and thermodynamic stability. Our screening identifies hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN) and boron carbide (B4C) as dual-function materials offering simultaneous mechanical protection and neutron radiation shielding, and we propose a graphene/h-BN/polymer layered heterostructure shield design that achieves an estimated 47% mass reduction relative to the original beryllium specification. These findings will become immediately actionable upon the successful development of fusion pulse propulsion, which we note remains an outstanding engineering challenge.

AI.tldr: This paperScreen systematically screen materials for an interstellar spacecraft radiation shield for Project Daedalus using DFT and GNN methods, identifying h-BN and B4C as dual-function materials and proposing a graphene/h-BN/polymer heterostructure that achieves 47% mass reduction.
AI.motivation: Project Daedalus (1973-1978) designed a 9 mm beryllium erosion shield for an interstellar probe, but this design predated modern materials like two-dimensional materials and GNN property predictors. Recent advances in computational materials science warrant reevaluation of the shield material using contemporary methods.
AI.method: The authors systematically screened 20 candidate materials spanning conventional aerospace metals, transition metal dichalcogenides, and ultra-high-temperature ceramics using density functional theory (DFT) data from the JARVIS database (76,000 materials) with independent validation by the Atomistic Line Graph Neural Network (ALIGNN). Candidates were evaluated across four criteria: specific mechanical stiffness (KV/rho), sputtering resistance, thermal neutron absorption cross-section, and thermodynamic stability.
AI.result: Hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN) and boron carbide (B4C) were identified as dual-function materials offering simultaneous mechanical protection and neutron radiation shielding. A graphene/h-BN/polymer layered heterostructure shield design was proposed, achieving an estimated 47% mass reduction relative to the original beryllium specification.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates the value of modern computational materials screening for aerospace applications. h-BN and B4C show promise as dual-function shield materials, and the proposed heterostructure design could significantly reduce spacecraft mass. However, the findings remain theoretical until fusion pulse propulsion is practically developed.

title: Nodal-Line Semimetals: Emerging Opportunities for Topological Electronics and Beyond
comment: 45 Pages, 12 figures with all relevant permissions obtained from the respective publications
summary: Topological semimetals have emerged as an important class of quantum materials with novel electronic responses and unconventional transport phenomena. Among them, nodal-line semimetals are distinguished by band crossings that extend along one-dimensional lines in momentum space rather than occurring at discrete points, forming closed loops, chains, or extended lines. The stability of these nodal structures is governed by crystalline symmetries such as mirror, spin-rotation, and nonsymmorphic operations, which give rise to characteristic topological invariants and surface states, including drumhead-like bands. In this review, we present a comprehensive overview of the theoretical framework and experimental realization of nodal-line semimetals, with particular emphasis on symmetry protection and the consequences of symmetry breaking. We discuss the classification of nodal-line structures, their evolution into other topological phases, and their signatures in electronic structure measurements and transport phenomena. Special attention is given to insights obtained from angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy and related probes. By bringing together symmetry analysis, band topology, and experimental observations, this review aims to clarify the relationship between topology, magnetism, and measurable electronic responses in nodal-line semimetals. These considerations highlight their potential as a versatile platform for next-generation topological electronic functionalities and emergent quantum phenomena beyond conventional paradigms.

AI.tldr: Review article on nodal-line semimetals, a class of topological quantum materials with band crossings extending along one-dimensional lines in momentum space, covering theoretical framework, experimental realization, symmetry protection, and potential applications.
AI.motivation: Nodal-line semimetals represent an important class of topological materials with unique electronic responses and transport phenomena. Their distinctive feature is band crossings forming closed loops, chains, or extended lines rather than discrete points. The field needed a comprehensive review to synthesize theoretical advances, experimental progress, and clarify relationships between topology, magnetism, and electronic responses.
AI.method: Comprehensive review combining symmetry analysis, band topology calculations, and experimental observations from angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES) and transport measurements. The paper examines classification of nodal-line structures, symmetry protection mechanisms (mirror, spin-rotation, nonsymmorphic operations), and phase transitions.
AI.result: Covers theoretical framework for nodal-line semimetals, identifies key symmetry protections (mirror, spin-rotation, nonsymmorphic operations), documents characteristic topological invariants and surface states (drumhead-like bands), discusses evolution into other topological phases, and presents signatures from electronic structure measurements and transport phenomena.
AI.conclusion: Nodal-line semimetals serve as a versatile platform for next-generation topological electronic functionalities and emergent quantum phenomena. The review clarifies relationships between topology, magnetism, and measurable electronic responses, highlighting potential applications beyond conventional paradigms.

title: Observation of acoustic magneto-chiral anisotropy in $α$-quartz
comment: 5 pages, 3 figures. Supplementary material available upon request
summary: We report the experimental observation of magneto-chiral anisotropy in the longitudinal and transverse ultrasound propagation in $α$-quartz. To perform such measurement, we have built an ultrasound spectrometer with unprecedented experimental resolution of the order of $Δv/v \sim 10^{-8}$. We present a simple macroscopic Becquerel-like analytical model that accounts for the magnitude of the observed effect and its frequency dependence.

AI.tldr: This paper reports the first experimental observation of magneto-chiral anisotropy (MChA) in longitudinal and transverse ultrasound propagation in α-quartz. The authors developed an ultrasound spectrometer with extremely high resolution (Δv/v ~ 10^-8) and used a macroscopic Becquerel-like analytical model to explain the observed effects and their frequency dependence.
AI.motivation: To experimentally observe magneto-chiral anisotropy (MChA) in acoustic wave propagation, which was theoretically predicted but never before experimentally confirmed in solids.
AI.method: Built an ultrasound spectrometer with unprecedented resolution (Δv/v ~ 10^-8) to measure magneto-chiral anisotropy in α-quartz using longitudinal and transverse ultrasound propagation.
AI.result: Measured the magneto-chiral anisotropy effect in α-quartz, with the magnitude and frequency dependence well explained by the macroscopic Becquerel-like analytical model.
AI.conclusion: First experimental observation of magneto-chiral anisotropy in both longitudinal and transverse ultrasound propagation in α-quartz, with a simple analytical model explaining the effect's magnitude and frequency dependence.

title: Strain-tunable multipiezo effects in Janus monolayer Cr2SSe: Selective reversal of valley polarization and single-spin-channel anomalous valley Hall effect
summary: Altermagnetism, the third class of collinear magnetic order, uniquely combines a zero net magnetization with spin polarized bands in reciprocal space, opening new avenues for two dimensional valleytronics and spintronics. Here, using first principles calculations, we predict that the Janus monolayer Cr2SSe, which possesses intrinsic inversion symmetry breaking, hosts a strain tunable multipiezo effect and exhibits distinctive valleytronic properties. The system displays pronounced spin splitting and band inversion at the X and Y high symmetry points in the Brillouin zone, giving rise to robust spin-valley locking. The degeneracy of these valleys is protected by diagonal mirror symmetry. Application of uniaxial strain breaks this symmetry, concurrently inducing piezovalley, piezoelectric, and piezomagnetic responses, a manifestation of the multipiezo effect. Critically, strain applied along orthogonal crystallographic directions yields opposite valley polarization, while under small compressive strain, we achieve selective reversal of valley polarization, enabling independent control of valence and conduction band valleys and promoting a single-spin-channel anomalous valley Hall effect. These findings establish a pathway for low-power, non volatile manipulation of valley degrees of freedom and enhanced spin transport efficiency, providing a theoretical foundation for the design of energy-efficient valleytronic devices.

AI.tldr: Using first principles calculations, this paper predicts that Janus monolayer Cr2SSe exhibits strain-tunable multipiezo effect with piezovalley, piezoelectric, and piezomagnetic responses. The system shows spin-valley locking at X and Y points, with strain enabling selective reversal of valley polarization and single-spin-channel anomalous valley Hall effect, offering potential for energy-efficient valleytronic devices.
AI.motivation: Altermagnetism represents the third class of collinear magnetic order, combining zero net magnetization with spin-polarized bands. The research aims to exploit these properties for valleytronic and spintronic applications. Specifically, the study focuses on Janus monolayer Cr2SSe which possesses intrinsic inversion symmetry breaking, aiming to achieve strain-tunable multipiezo effect and distinctive valleytronic properties.
AI.method: First principles calculations were employed to investigate the electronic and magnetic properties of Janus monolayer Cr2SSe.
AI.result: The system exhibits pronounced spin splitting and band inversion at X and Y high symmetry points in the Brillouin zone, giving rise to robust spin-valley locking protected by diagonal mirror symmetry. Uniaxial strain breaks this symmetry and induces piezovalley, piezoelectric, and piezomagnetic responses (multipiezo effect). Strain along orthogonal directions yields opposite valley polarization. Under small compressive strain, selective reversal of valley polarization enables independent control of valence and conduction band valleys and promotes a single-spin-channel anomalous valley Hall effect.
AI.conclusion: This work establishes a pathway for low-power, non-volatile manipulation of valley degrees of freedom and enhanced spin transport efficiency, providing a theoretical foundation for designing energy-efficient valleytronic devices.

title: Spontaneous structural reconstructions and properties of ultrathin triangular ZnSe nanoplatelets
summary: Two-dimensional (2D) materials have revolutionized all areas of development of high-performance electronic devices. In particular, the unique electronic and optical properties of II--VI semiconductor nanoplatelets have been found to be very promising for optoelectronics. However, not all properties of this intriguing class of materials are yet known. A new, previously unknown hexagonal 2D structure of ZnSe nanoplatelets whose energy is lower than the energies of all previously studied systems is found from first-principles calculations. This structure appears as a result of spontaneous reconstruction of the wurtzite structure and differs from it by the stacking order of the bulk and near-surface Zn atomic layers. The phonon spectrum, electronic structure, and band gap of the obtained nanoplatelets are calculated. The phonon spectra of the nanoplatelets are in complete agreement with the spectra observed in experiment and differ strongly from the vibrational spectra of ZnSe nanoclusters. The adsorption of ZnCl$_2$ and $L$-cysteine molecules on the surface of the nanoplatelets is studied and is shown to be accompanied by yet another spontaneous reconstruction of the hexagonal structure into a tetragonal one and a new rearrangement of Zn atoms in the near-surface layers. Calculations of the natural optical activity of nanoplatelets covered with $L$-cysteine reveal an increase in the specific (calculated per chiral molecule) optical activity, which is especially strong for the Janus structures, as compared to the free $L$-cysteine molecule.

AI.tldr: First-principles calculations discovered a new stable hexagonal 2D ZnSe nanoplatelet structure lower in energy than previous systems, arising from wurtzite reconstruction. Phonon spectra match experimental data. Adsorption of ZnCl2 and L-cysteine triggers further reconstruction to tetragonal phase, with L-cysteine functionalization enhancing optical activity, particularly in Janus structures.
