AI.motivation: II-VI semiconductor nanoplatelets (particularly ZnSe) show promising optoelectronic properties, but not all properties of this class of materials are fully understood. The study aimed to discover new stable 2D structures and understand their properties.
AI.method: First-principles calculations including phonon spectrum analysis, electronic structure calculations, band gap calculations, and optical activity computations
AI.result:
1) Discovered a new hexagonal 2D ZnSe nanoplatelet structure with energy lower than all previously studied systems, arising from spontaneous wurtzite reconstruction
2) Calculated phonon spectra, electronic structure, and band gap - phonon spectra agree with experiments and differ from ZnSe nanoclusters
3) Adsorption of ZnCl2 and L-cysteine causes another spontaneous reconstruction to tetragonal structure with Zn atom rearrangement
4) L-cysteine coated nanoplatelets show increased specific optical activity, especially for Janus structures compared to free L-cysteine
AI.conclusion: The discovery of this new hexagonal 2D ZnSe structure with lower energy than previously known systems represents a significant advance in understanding 2D materials. The spontaneous reconstruction phenomena, combined with enhanced optical activity when functionalized with chiral molecules, suggests promising applications in optoelectronics and chiroptical devices. The agreement between calculated and experimental phonon spectra validates the computational approach.

title: Microscopic Theory of Superionic Phase Transitions: Nonadiabatic Dynamics and Many-Body Effects
summary: Superionic phase transitions have attracted extensive interest for decades due to their promising applications and rich underlying physics. In particular, complicated many-body effects and nonadiabatic dynamics are believed to play essential roles, limiting the explanatory power of phenomenological approaches and obscuring the microscopic mechanisms at play. In this work, we develop a unified theoretical framework for describing solid-state ionic conduction. After reviewing the conventional approximations, we construct a general lattice model that applies to both normal ionic and superionic conductors. By incorporating the nonadiabatic concerted-hopping mechanism and the many-body Coulomb interaction within a self-consistent mean-field scheme, we identify these two effects as the fundamental driving forces behind type-I and type-II superionic phase transitions, respectively. Our model directly reproduces key experimental observations. Within this unified framework, we further provide a comprehensive comparison between the two types of transitions. Overall, our work offers microscopic insight into superionic phase transitions and provides guidance for the design and optimization of advanced solid-state ionic conductors.

AI.tldr: A unified theoretical framework for solid-state ionic conduction that identifies nonadiabatic concerted-hopping and many-body Coulomb interactions as the two fundamental driving forces behind type-I and type-II superionic phase transitions, respectively.
AI.motivation: Superionic phase transitions have promising applications and rich physics, but complicated many-body effects and nonadiabatic dynamics limit the explanatory power of phenomenological approaches and obscure microscopic mechanisms.
AI.method: Developed a unified theoretical framework by constructing a general lattice model applicable to both normal ionic and superionic conductors. Incorporated the nonadiabatic concerted-hopping mechanism and many-body Coulomb interaction within a self-consistent mean-field scheme.
AI.result: Identified nonadiabatic concerted-hopping and many-body Coulomb interaction as the fundamental driving forces behind type-I and type-II superionic phase transitions, respectively. The model directly reproduces key experimental observations.
AI.conclusion: Provides microscopic insight into superionic phase transitions and offers guidance for the design and optimization of advanced solid-state ionic conductors.

title: A comparison of the spin-phonon behaviour of Fe$_2$P-based magnetocaloric materials
summary:
Magnetic refrigeration can provide an environmentally friendly technology to reduce significantly the energy consumption of cooling devices. To retain the sustainability of the device, all parts must be made from abundant materials, excluding e.g. rare earth elements. As such, materials based on Fe$_2$P have shown great potential for magnetocaloric devices. In this study, Fe$_2$P and FeMnP$_{0.55}$Si$_{0.45}$, have been studied using magnetometry, neutron scattering and theoretical modelling with the aim to understand the ferromagnetic transition, related to the magnetocaloric effect. Analysis of the diffraction data of Fe$_2$P showed that it is the Fe$_{3g}$-site that drives the magnetic transition as the Fe$_{3f}$ does not have any magnetic contribution at the magnetic transition temperature. For FeMnP$_{0.55}$Si$_{0.45}$, the magnetic transition is more gradual, on both sites, with coexistence of the para- and ferromagnetic phases close to the magnetic transition. The temperature dependent magnetic structure behaviour are well in agreement with our first principles calculations.
  Both Fe$_2$P and FeMnP$_{0.55}$Si$_{0.45}$ showed two distinct regions, at different length scales, in their S(\textbf{Q},$ω$) spectra. The two length scales can be modelled using a different set of magnetic spin states (S), using S$\rm _{Fe}$~=~2 and S$\rm _{Mn}$~=~2.5, consistent with the ground state of the magnetic atoms. QENS at low Q (Q~\textless{}~0.5~Å) shows similar magnetic processes in both compounds with uncorrelated magnetism below the magnetic transition temperature. The uncorrelated state highlights that the magnetic anisotropy does not play a major role in the formation of the magnetic state. Furthermore, this emphasises the existence of a two part system in FeMn(P,Si)-based compounds, that drives the magnetic transition and in turn the magnetocaloric effect.

AI.tldr: Study of Fe₂P and FeMnP₀.₅₅Si₀.₄₅ for magnetocaloric applications reveals Fe₃g-site drives transition in Fe₂P, while the Mn-doped compound shows gradual transition with phase coexistence. Both exhibit two-length-scale magnetic dynamics with uncorrelated magnetism, confirming FeMn(P,Si)-based materials are promising rare-earth-free candidates for magnetic refrigeration.
AI.motivation: Develop sustainable magnetic refrigeration technology using abundant materials (excluding rare earth elements) for energy-efficient cooling devices
AI.method: Magnetometry, neutron scattering (diffraction and QENS), and first-principles calculations
AI.result: Fe₂P: Fe₃g-site drives magnetic transition while Fe₃f-site shows no magnetic contribution. FeMnP₀.₅₅Si₀.₄₅: gradual transition with para-ferromagnetic phase coexistence. Both show two distinct length scales in S(Q,ω) spectra with S_Fe~2 and S_Mn~2.5. Low-Q QENS reveals uncorrelated magnetism below T_C, indicating magnetic anisotropy does not play a major role.
AI.conclusion: The study confirms Fe₂P-based materials are promising for magnetocaloric devices without rare earth elements. Fe₃g-site drives the magnetic transition in Fe₂P, while FeMnP₀.₅₅Si₀.₄₅ shows gradual transition with para-ferromagnetic phase coexistence. The two-length-scale magnetic behavior suggests a two-part system driving the magnetocaloric effect, with uncorrelated magnetism below T_C indicating magnetic anisotropy is not dominant.

title: First principles study of thermoelectric properties of $\text{Nb}_2\text{Co}_2\text{InSb}$ and $\text{Nb}_2\text{Co}_2\text{GaSb}$ double half-Heuslers
summary:
Valence electron count (VEC) 18 half-Heusler (hH) alloys are considered promising for high-temperature thermoelectric applications due to their high Seebeck coefficient, mechanical stability, and robustness. However, their relatively large lattice thermal conductivity ($k_{L}$) significantly limits their thermoelectric performance. Introducing mass disorder at lattice sites is an effective approach to suppress $k_{L}$ through enhanced phonon scattering. For instance, NbCoSn exhibits a low figure of merit ($zT \sim 0.05$) despite having a reasonably high power factor of 2.1~$\text{mW}/\text{mK}^{2}$ at room temperature, mainly due to its large lattice thermal conductivity, reported to be 13.25~$\text{W}/\text{mK}$ experimentally and 18~$\text{W}/\text{mK}$ theoretically.
  In this work, we explore the thermoelectric properties of $\text{Nb}_2\text{Co}_2\text{InSb}$ and $\text{Nb}_2\text{Co}_2\text{GaSb}$, which can be regarded as derivatives of NbCoSn with substitution at the Sn site. Both ordered and Special Quasirandom Structures (SQSs) are considered to understand the role of configurational disorder. Energetic analysis indicates that the ordered phase is most stable for $\text{Nb}_2\text{Co}_2\text{InSb}$, whereas the SQS phase is energetically favored for $\text{Nb}_2\text{Co}_2\text{GaSb}$. The lattice thermal conductivity is evaluated using the Debye-Callaway model, yielding values in the range of 5.5-6.9~$\text{W}/\text{mK}$ for $\text{Nb}_2\text{Co}_2\text{InSb}$ and 4.7-5.8~$\text{W}/\text{mK}$ for $\text{Nb}_2\text{Co}_2\text{GaSb}$ at room temperature. These values are significantly lower than those of the parent NbCoSn system, highlighting the effectiveness of mass disorder in reducing thermal conductivity. The results suggest that these double half-Heusler compounds are promising candidates for improved thermoelectric performance.

AI.tldr: This study explores Nb2Co2InSb and Nb2Co2GaSb as derivatives of NbCoSn half-Heusler alloys for thermoelectric applications. Using Debye-Callaway model calculations, researchers found significantly reduced lattice thermal conductivity (4.7-6.9 W/mK) compared to parent NbCoSn (13.25-18 W/mK), making these double half-Heusler compounds promising candidates for high-temperature thermoelectric devices.
AI.motivation: The high lattice thermal conductivity (kL) in 18 VEC half-Heusler alloys limits their thermoelectric performance despite favorable Seebeck coefficients and power factors. This work aims to reduce kL through mass disorder introduced by substitution at the Sn site in NbCoSn.
AI.method: Energetic analysis using both ordered and Special Quasirandom Structures (SQSs) to understand configurational disorder effects. Lattice thermal conductivity evaluated using the Debye-Callaway model.
AI.result: Nb2Co2InSb shows ordered phase stability with kL of 5.5-6.9 W/mK at room temperature. Nb2Co2GaSb favors SQS phase with kL of 4.7-5.8 W/mK. Both values are significantly lower than NbCoSn's 13.25-18 W/mK.
AI.conclusion: Mass disorder via site substitution effectively reduces lattice thermal conductivity. Nb2Co2InSb and Nb2Co2GaSb show promising thermoelectric performance improvements over parent NbCoSn and warrant further investigation for high-temperature applications.

title: Laser-generated CuPdAgPtAu High-Entropy Alloy Nanoparticles -- Thermal Segregation Threshold and Elemental Segregation
comment: submitted to Particles and Particle Systems Characterization
summary: High-entropy alloy nanoparticles synthesized via laser ablation in liquid are promising for catalysis due to their ability to form simple solid solutions despite chemical complexity. In this study, noble metal HEA NPs (CuPdAgPtAu) are produced from equimolar and Cu- or Ag-enriched bulk targets. Advanced electron microscopy, XRD, and atomistic simulations are used for structural and compositional analysis. Equimolar targets and NPs exhibit a single fcc phase. In contrast, Cu- or Ag-enriched targets show phase segregation into two fcc phases, which is not observed in the synthesized NPs. Simulations predict segregation tendencies, including Ag surface enrichment and Pt core enrichment due to surface energy differences. However, experimentally, individual NPs remain compositionally homogeneous. Thermal stability studies reveal that phase segregation can be induced post-synthesis. Upon heating, Cu-Ag segregation occurs, forming a second fcc phase similar to bulk targets. These findings demonstrate that rapid quenching during laser ablation suppresses thermodynamically driven segregation and stabilizes metastable solid solutions under kinetic control. Subsequent slow heating overcomes kinetic barriers, enabling equilibrium phase formation at higher temperatures. The thermal stability of these NPs and their tunable composition, including Cu enrichment beyond equilibrium limits, make them promising for high-temperature catalytic applications while reducing noble metal usage.

AI.tldr: High-entropy alloy nanoparticles (CuPdAgPtAu) synthesized via laser ablation in liquid form metastable solid solutions due to rapid quenching, despite bulk targets showing phase segregation. Post-synthesis heating induces phase segregation, demonstrating kinetic control over thermodynamic equilibrium.
AI.motivation: High-entropy alloy nanoparticles are promising for catalysis due to their ability to form simple solid solutions despite chemical complexity. This study investigates the synthesis and thermal stability of noble metal HEA nanoparticles.
AI.method: Laser ablation in liquid synthesis with advanced electron microscopy, XRD, and atomistic simulations for structural and compositional analysis, plus thermal stability studies.
AI.result: Equimolar targets/NPs show single fcc phase, while Cu/Ag-enriched targets show phase segregation. Synthesized NPs remain compositionally homogeneous due to rapid quenching. Simulations predict Ag surface enrichment and Pt core enrichment. Post-synthesis heating induces Cu-Ag segregation similar to bulk behavior.
AI.conclusion: Rapid quenching during laser ablation suppresses thermodynamically driven segregation and stabilizes metastable solid solutions. Subsequent slow heating overcomes kinetic barriers, enabling equilibrium phase formation. These findings demonstrate tunable thermal stability and composition for high-temperature catalytic applications while reducing noble metal usage.

title: Cryogenic stabilization of molecular hydrogen in dense cubic ice
summary: Hydrogen is widely regarded as a cornerstone of future low-carbon energy technologies, yet the lack of safe, efficient, and reversible solid-state storage materials remains a major barrier to its large-scale deployment. Although porous frameworks and metal hydrides have been extensively explored, far less is known about the ability of dense molecular solids to stabilize hydrogen at near-ambient pressure. Here we show that fully crystalline cubic ice, despite its non-porous nature, can retain molecular hydrogen as an interstitial guest following controlled decompression from a high-pressure hydrogen hydrate precursor. Using synchrotron X-ray diffraction, neutron diffraction, and Raman spectroscopy, we demonstrate that hydrogen is retained within the ice structure up to about 130 K, producing reproducible lattice expansion and distinct spectroscopic signatures. We further show that pure cubic ice can be partially refilled with hydrogen at 0.18 GPa and 130 K, while fully hydrogen-filled cubic structure can be preserved at the same pressure up to 90 K. The retained hydrogen content reaches several percent of the parent hydrate composition, corresponding to gravimetric and volumetric storage densities comparable to those of interstitial hydrogen in metals. These results reveal an unexpected ability of a dense hydrogen-bonded crystal structure to host molecular hydrogen without permanent porosity or chemical bonding, establishing cubic ice as a minimal model for hydrogen-lattice interactions. More broadly, our findings identify dense hydrogen-bonded solids as an unexplored class of materials for hydrogen storage physics, with implications extending from energy materials to planetary and astrophysical ice environments.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates that cubic ice (a dense, non-porous molecular solid) can retain molecular hydrogen as an interstitial guest following decompression from a high-pressure hydrogen hydrate precursor, achieving storage densities comparable to metals - establishing a new class of hydrogen storage materials.
AI.motivation: Hydrogen is crucial for future low-carbon energy, but safe and efficient solid-state storage remains a major barrier. While porous frameworks and metal hydrides have been studied, dense molecular solids have been largely unexplored for hydrogen storage at near-ambient conditions.
AI.method: The researchers used synchrotron X-ray diffraction, neutron diffraction, and Raman spectroscopy to characterize hydrogen retention in cubic ice after controlled decompression from high-pressure hydrogen hydrate precursors.
AI.result: Cubic ice can retain molecular hydrogen up to ~130 K, showing lattice expansion and distinct spectroscopic signatures. Pure cubic ice can be partially refilled with hydrogen at 0.18 GPa and 130 K, and fully hydrogen-filled cubic structure can be preserved at the same pressure up to 90 K. The retained hydrogen reaches several percent of parent hydrate composition, with gravimetric and volumetric storage densities comparable to interstitial hydrogen in metals.
AI.conclusion: Dense hydrogen-bonded crystals like cubic ice can host molecular hydrogen without permanent porosity or chemical bonding, establishing cubic ice as a minimal model for hydrogen-lattice interactions. This identifies dense hydrogen-bonded solids as an unexplored class of hydrogen storage materials, with implications for energy materials, planetary science, and astrophysics.

title: Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning of Machine-Learning Interatomic Potentials for Phonon and Thermal Properties
summary: Machine-learning interatomic potentials are widely used as computationally efficient surrogates for density functional theory in atomistic simulations, enabling large-scale, long-time modeling of materials systems. We investigate how different fine-tuning strategies influence the prediction of harmonic phonon band structures, thermal properties, and the potential energy surface along imaginary phonon modes. We achieve substantial accuracy improvements with minimal additional data, with as few as 10 additional training structures already yielding significant gains. In addition to existing approaches, we introduce Equitrain, a finetuning framework that implements LoRA-based adaptation. Across 53 materials systems, we show that fine-tuned models consistently outperform both the underlying pretrained model and models trained from scratch. Equitrain achieves the best overall performance, and our results demonstrate that fine-tuning enables accurate phonon predictions.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates fine-tuning strategies for machine-learning interatomic potentials to predict phonon properties. The proposed Equitrain framework using LoRA adaptation achieves superior performance across 53 materials systems, requiring only 10 additional training structures for significant accuracy gains in phonon band structures, thermal properties, and energy surface predictions.
AI.motivation: Machine-learning interatomic potentials serve as computationally efficient surrogates for density functional theory in atomistic simulations, enabling large-scale modeling of materials systems. However, their accuracy for specific properties like phonon calculations needed improvement, prompting investigation into optimal fine-tuning strategies.
AI.method: The study compares different fine-tuning strategies including the newly introduced Equitrain framework that implements LoRA-based adaptation. The researchers evaluated fine-tuned models on harmonic phonon band structures, thermal properties, and potential energy surfaces along imaginary phonon modes across 53 materials systems.
AI.result: Substantial accuracy improvements were achieved with minimal additional data - as few as 10 additional training structures yielded significant gains. Fine-tuned models consistently outperformed both the underlying pretrained model and models trained from scratch. Equitrain achieved the best overall performance among all approaches tested.
AI.conclusion: Fine-tuning with as few as 10 additional structures enables substantial accuracy improvements in phonon predictions. Equitrain, the proposed LoRA-based framework, achieves the best overall performance across 53 materials systems, consistently outperforming both pretrained base models and models trained from scratch. This demonstrates that fine-tuning is a practical and effective approach for achieving accurate phonon predictions with minimal additional computational cost.
